revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Dō (architecture)]][[fr:Dō (architecture)]][[id:Fotang]][[ja:仏堂]][[sl:Do (arhitektura)]][[zh:佛堂]]
{{Short description|Traditional Japanese buildings|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}} [[File:Kyoto Toji Mieido C0973.jpg|thumb|A ''miei-dō'']] {{Nihongo|'''''Dō'''''|堂||temple, shrine, hall, reception room; also shop, store<ref>Nelson, A.N., The Modern Reader's Japanese-English Dictionary, {{ISBN|0-8048-0408-7|qq=}}</ref>|qq=}}. It is very often used in {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}} as a suffix in the name of some of the many buildings that can be part of a Japanese [[:en:Garan|temple compound]]. (Other endings, for example -''den'' as in ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'', exist.) The suffix can be the name of a deity associated with it (e.g. ''Yakushi-dō'', a name customarily translated as "[[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]] Hall") or express the building's function within the temple's compound (e.g. ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=hon-dō|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'', or main hall).<ref group="note">Hall names are capitalized only when they refer to specific examples (e.g. XX-ji's Main Hall) or include proper names of deities (e.g. Yakushi-dō).</ref> Some words ending in -''dō'' are ''Butsu-dō'', ''hō-dō'', ''hon-dō'', ''jiki-dō'', ''kaisan-dō'', ''kō-dō'', ''kon-dō'', ''kyō-dō'', ''mandara-dō'', ''miei-dō'', ''mi-dō'', ''sō-dō'', ''Yakushi-dō'' and ''zen-dō''. With some exceptions, for example the words ''hondō'', ''hokke-dō'' and ''kon-dō'', these terms do not indicate any particular structure. The suffix is used sometimes also in a lay context, as for example in the word {{Nihongo|''shokudō''|食堂||lit. food building, meaning restaurant or cafeteria|qq=}}. A ''dō's'' size is measured in ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|qid=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}'', where a ''ken'' is the interval between two pillars of a traditional-style building. A ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'' for example is a 9x7 ''ken''.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kondou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kondou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=6 September 2010|qq=}}</ref> The word is usually translated in English as "bay" and is better understood as an indication of proportions than as a unit of measurement. ==Types== *''Amida-dō'' (阿弥陀堂) – a building that enshrines a statue of [[Amitābha|Amida]].<ref name="jaanus"/> *''daishi-dō'' (大師堂) – lit. "great master hall". A building dedicated to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kōbō Daishi|1=Kūkai}} ([[Shingon]]) or [[Saichō|Dengyō Daishi]] ({{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}}).<ref name="jaanus"/> *''hattō*'' (法堂) – lit. ''{{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|qid=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}'' hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the ''hō'').<ref name="kojien">Iwanami {{Nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|qid=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}}|広辞苑|qq=}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> *''hō-dō'' (法堂) – see ''hattō''. *''hokke-dō*'' (法華堂) – lit. "Lotus Sūtra hall". In {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} Buddhism, a hall whose layout allows walking around a statue for meditation.<ref name="jaanus">{{Cite web|title= Corresponding JAANUS article|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/|publisher=JAANUS|qq=}}</ref> The purpose of walking is to concentrate on the ''{{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=Lotus Sũtra|1=Lotus Sutra}}'' and seek the ultimate truth.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''[[:en:Hon-dō|Hon-dō]]*'' (本堂) – lit. "main hall", it is the building that houses the most important statues and objects of cult.<ref name="jaanus"/> The term is thought to have evolved to avoid the term ''kon-dō'' used by six Nara sects (the ''{{ill|Nanto Rokushū|en|Nanto Rokushū|qid=Q5366159|lt=Nanto Rokushū|1=Nanto Rokushū}}'')<ref name="jaanus"/> for their main halls. Structurally similar, but its inner less strictly defined. *''jiki-dō*'' (食堂) – a monastery's refectory.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''kaisan-dō'' (開山堂) – founder's hall, usually at a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} temple. Building enshrining a statue, portrait or memorial tablet of the founder of either the temple or the sect it belongs to. [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo sect]] temples often call it ''miei-dō''.<ref name="jaanus"/> * ''kō-dō*'' (講堂) – lecture hall of a non-Zen ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]''.<ref name="kojien"/> *''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}*'' (金堂) – lit. "golden hall", it is the main hall of a ''garan'', housing the main object of worship.<ref name="jaanus"/> Unlike a ''butsuden'', it is a true two-story building (although the second story may sometimes be missing) which measures 9x7 bays.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''kyō-dō'' (経堂) – see ''[[kyōzō]]''. *''[[kyōzō]]'' (経蔵) – lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}}s and books about the temple's history.<ref name="jaanus"/> Also called ''kyō–dō''. *''mandara-dō'' (曼荼羅堂) – lit. "hall of [[Mandala#Buddhism|mandalas]]", but the name is now used only for {{ill|Taima-dera|en|Taima-dera|qid=Q478979|lt=Taima-dera|1=Taima-dera}}'s Main Hall in Nara.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''miei-dō*'' (御影堂) – lit. "image hall". Building housing an image of the temple's founder, equivalent to a Zen sect's ''kaisan-dō''.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''mi-dō'' (御堂) – a generic honorific term for a building which enshrines a sacred statue.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''rokkaku-dō'' (六角堂) – a hexagonal temple building. An example of this type of structure gives its nickname to Kyoto's {{ill|Rokkaku-dō|en|Rokkaku-dō|qid=Q599378|lt=Chōhō-ji|1=Rokkaku-dō}}, better known as Rokkaku-dō. *''shaka-dō'' (釈迦堂) – lit. [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] hall. A building enshrining a statue of Buddha.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''sō-dō*'' (僧堂) – lit. "monk hall". A building dedicated to the practice of ''{{ill|Zazen|en|Zazen|qid=Q167894|lt=Zazen|1=Zazen}}''.<ref name="kojien"/> It used to be dedicated to various activities, from eating to sleeping, centered on ''zazen''. *''soshi-dō'' (祖師堂) – lit. "patriarchs hall". A building dedicated to the ''soshi'', important teachers and priests.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''Yakushi-dō*'' (薬師堂) – a building that enshrines a statue of [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]] Nyorai.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''{{ill|Zendō|en|Zendō|qid=Q642574|lt=Zendō|1=Zendō}}*'' (禅堂) – lit. "hall of Zen".<ref name="jaanus"/> The building where monks practice ''zazen'', and one of the main structures of a Zen ''garan''.<ref name="jaanus"/> ==Notes== <references group="note"/> ==References== {{Reflist|qq=}} {{Japanese architectural elements|qq=}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan|qq=}}
[[en:Dō (architecture)]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Dō (architecture)}} [[fr:Dō (architecture)]] [[ja:仏堂]] [[sl:Do (arhitektura)]] [[zh:佛堂]] {{wikidata link|Q11377515}} [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
